name: Mont Saint-Michel
summary: Mont Saint-Michel is a tidal island and mainland commune in Normandy, France, located approximately one kilometer off the country's northwestern coast. The island has been a strategic stronghold since ancient times and is the site of a medieval monastery dedicated to the Archangel Michael. Rising dramatically from the tidal flats, its Gothic spire crowned by a golden statue of St. Michael makes it one of the most recognizable landmarks in France, attracting over three million visitors annually.

timeline.title: Aubert's Vision and First Chapel
timeline.description: According to legend, the Archangel Michael appears to Aubert, Bishop of Avranches, three times commanding him to build a church on the rocky island. Aubert constructs a small chapel on the mount, establishing the first religious site. A hole in the rock said to have been made by the angel's finger is still visible today.

timeline.title: Benedictine Abbey Founded
timeline.description: Duke Richard I of Normandy installs a community of Benedictine monks on the island, establishing the Abbey of Mont Saint-Michel. The monks follow the Rule of St. Benedict and begin an ambitious building program, transforming the small chapel into a major monastic center that becomes one of the most important pilgrimage sites in Christendom.

timeline.title: Romanesque Church Construction
timeline.description: Abbot Hildebert II begins construction of a massive Romanesque church at the summit of the mount, building over the existing chapel. The extraordinary engineering challenge of constructing on the narrow rocky peak requires innovative solutions, with the crypt and supporting structures creating the foundation for the soaring church above.

timeline.title: French King's Siege and Fire
timeline.description: King Philip II of France attempts to seize the abbey from Normandy. During the siege, a fire destroys the Romanesque church and much of the monastery. Philip compensates the monks with funds to rebuild, inadvertently launching the construction of the magnificent Gothic structures known as 'La Merveille' that define the mount today.

timeline.title: Hundred Years War Siege
timeline.description: During the Hundred Years War, English forces besiege Mont Saint-Michel but fail to capture it despite a 25-year blockade. The abbey's natural defenses and fortifications prove impregnable, and the mount becomes a powerful symbol of French resistance. Only about 50 French soldiers defend it against repeated English assaults.

timeline.title: Revolutionary Prison
timeline.description: After the French Revolution, the abbey is converted into a state prison, known as the 'Bastille of the Sea,' holding political and common criminals. The prison operates for nearly 70 years, causing significant damage to the medieval buildings. Victor Hugo campaigns for its closure, calling it a desecration of the monument.

timeline.title: UNESCO World Heritage Designation
timeline.description: Mont Saint-Michel is designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site for its architectural and historical significance. This triggers major conservation efforts and eventually leads to an ambitious 1.8 billion euro project to restore the maritime character of the mount by removing the causeway and replacing it with a bridge, allowing the tides to flow freely once again.

images.caption: Bishop Aubert's vision of the Archangel Michael commanding him to build a chapel on the rocky island
The construction of the Gothic 'La Merveille' following the fire that destroyed the Romanesque church
The mount withstanding the English siege during the Hundred Years War, its fortifications unbreached
The fully restored Mont Saint-Michel with the new bridge allowing tidal waters to surround the island once more
